metadata.title: Why Your Resume Is Killing Your Huntington 100 Nomination

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The selection committee is instructed to judge only what the nomination form literally states. They will not Google you, so any accomplishment left implied simply goes unseen. Resumes fail here because they gesture at impact instead of naming concrete actions. The fix is answering plainly what you were literally doing, spelled out in full.

artifactLines: The selection committee is instructed to judge only what the nomination form literally states.
They will not Google you, so any accomplishment left implied simply goes unseen.
Resumes fail here because they gesture at impact instead of naming concrete actions.
The fix is answering plainly what you were literally doing, spelled out in full.

beats.narration_text: The fact that the selection committee is explicitly told to ignore additional information and inferences beyond the nomination form flipped how I think about self-presentation. I want to rewrite accordingly. Help me: (1) explain why a resume's implied accomplishments fail when nobody will Google you, (2) turn a vague resume bullet into a concrete what were you literally doing statement, and (3) draft one nomination sentence that survives a reader allowed to infer nothing.

output: Student: Hey Professor, I want a nomination for Huntington 100
Look, I don’t run this program
They’re not going to Google you
